message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To explore the patterns of sickness absence in National Health Service (NHS) staff attributable to mental ill health during the first wave of the Covid-19 epidemic in March – July 2020</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Case-referent analysis of a secondary data set</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>NHS Trusts in England</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Pseudonymised data on 959,356 employees who were continuously employed by NHS trusts during 1 January 2019 to 31 July 2020</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome Measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Trends in the burden of sickness absence due to mental ill health from 2019 to 2020 according to demographic, regional and occupational characteristics.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Over the study period, 164,202 new sickness absence episodes for mental ill health were recorded in 12.5% (119,525) of the study sample. There was a spike of sickness absence for mental ill health in March-April 2020 (899,730 days lost) compared with 519,807 days in March-April 2019; the surge was driven by an increase in new episodes of long-term absence and had diminished by May/June 2020. The increase was greatest in those aged >60 years (227%) and among employees of Asian and Black ethnic origin (109%-136%). Among doctors and dentists the number of days absent declined by 12.7%. The biggest increase was in London (122%) and the smallest in the East Midlands (43.7%); the variation between regions reflected the rates of Covid-19 sickness absence during the same period.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Although the Covid-19 epidemic led to an increase in sickness absence attributed to mental ill health in NHS staff, this had substantially declined by May/June 2020, corresponding with the decrease in pressures at work as the first wave of the epidemic subsided.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Article Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:bold>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:bold>
                  </jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Large study population</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Study population were not self-selected</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Job exposure matrix allowed adjustment for occupational exposure</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Data did not extend to the start of the second wave in September 2020</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: IMPACT OF COVID-19 PANDEMIC ON SICKNESS ABSENCE FOR MENTAL ILL HEALTH IN NATIONAL HEALTH SERVICE STAFF
